subtopics: Identify the forces acting in a given situation, including drawing force diagrams
Understand the vector nature of force, and find and use components and resultants
Use the principle of equilibrium: vector sum of forces is zero, or sum of components in any direction is zero
Understand that a contact force between two surfaces has normal and frictional components
Use the model of a 'smooth' contact and understand its limitations
Understand limiting friction and limiting equilibrium, and use the coefficient of friction F = μR
Use Newton's third law (action-reaction pairs)
topics.description: Candidates should identify forces, understand vector components and resultants, apply equilibrium principles including resolving forces, use friction models (smooth and rough with coefficient of friction), and apply Newton's third law.

topics.topicName: Kinematics of motion in a straight line
subtopics: Understand distance and speed as scalar quantities, and displacement, velocity and acceleration as vector quantities
Sketch and interpret displacement-time graphs and velocity-time graphs
Use the relationships between gradient of displacement-time graph and velocity, gradient of velocity-time graph and acceleration, and area under velocity-time graph and displacement
Use differentiation and integration with respect to time to solve problems concerning displacement, velocity and acceleration
Use appropriate formulae for motion with constant acceleration in a straight line (SUVAT equations)
topics.description: Candidates should understand scalar and vector kinematic quantities, interpret and sketch displacement-time and velocity-time graphs, use calculus (differentiation and integration) with respect to time, and apply SUVAT equations for motion with constant acceleration.

subtopics: Use the definition of linear momentum and show understanding of its vector nature
Use conservation of linear momentum to solve problems modelled as the direct impact of two bodies
Apply conservation of momentum to problems where bodies coalesce on impact
topics.description: Candidates should use the definition of linear momentum, understand its vector nature (in one dimension), and apply conservation of linear momentum to direct impact problems including cases where bodies coalesce.

subtopics: Apply Newton's laws of motion to linear motion of a particle under constant forces including friction, tension and thrust
Use the relationship between mass and weight (W = mg) with g ≈ 10 m/s²
Solve problems involving motion of a particle on an inclined plane with constant acceleration
Solve problems involving motion of connected particles, including light inextensible strings over smooth pulleys and towing problems
topics.description: Candidates should apply Newton's three laws to linear motion problems involving constant forces (friction, tension, thrust), use W = mg, solve inclined plane problems with varying acceleration directions, and solve connected particle problems involving pulleys and tow-bars.

subtopics: Understand the concept of work done by a force and calculate work done by a constant force (W = Fd cos θ)
Understand gravitational potential energy and kinetic energy, and use appropriate formulae
Understand and use the relationship between change in energy and work done by external forces, and apply the principle of conservation of energy
Use the definition of power as the rate of doing work, and the relationship P = Fv for a force acting in the direction of motion
Solve problems involving instantaneous acceleration of a body moving on a hill against resistance
topics.description: Candidates should calculate work done by forces, use kinetic and gravitational potential energy formulae, apply the work-energy principle and conservation of energy, define and calculate power including P = Fv, and solve resistance and inclined plane energy problems.
